title: The corner that gets cut
checkLabels.t12: The heading is still dark red
why.empty: The editor is empty. Hit the bin and start over.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. The corners are still sharp.
hint2.untouched: One new line in each of the two rules.
why.unlinked: The page cannot find styles.css. You changed the link line.
hint2.unlinked: Hit the bin and start over.
why.used-inline: You used style in the tag. That is lesson 01 again.
why.touched-html: You edited the HTML. Here you work only in styles.css.
why.no-card: There is no element with the card class.
why.no-badge: There is no element with the badge class.
why.card-square: The card corners are still sharp.
hint2.card-square: The property is border-radius and it takes a length.
why.card-partial: You rounded only some of the corners.
hint2.card-partial: One number covers all four. With four numbers the order runs clockwise again, but starting from the top LEFT corner.
why.badge-square: The badge is still a square.
hint2.badge-square: In pixels you would have to calculate. There is a value that means half the side, whatever the side is.
hint3.badge-square: A percentage is measured against the sides of the box. At 50 percent every corner curves to the midpoint — and the square becomes a circle.
why.badge-resized: You changed the size of the badge. It stays 64 by 64.
hint2.badge-resized: The circle comes from the percentage, not from the size. Change one side and you get an ellipse.
why.h1-broken: The heading is no longer dark red.
blocks.0.text: The card and the badge have sharp corners. Nothing is broken — they just look like something made in 2004. A rounded corner is one line.
blocks.4.text: The name is misleading. border-radius does not need a border and works the same without one — it cuts the corner of the whole box, along with the background and everything inside. The border, if there is one, simply follows the new shape. The number is the radius of the arc: the bigger it is, the rounder the corner. Eight pixels reads as intentional without shouting; twenty is already a different kind of design.
blocks.6.text: For a round badge you do not count pixels:
blocks.8.text: A percentage is measured against the sides of the box itself — the horizontal radius against the width, the vertical one against the height. At 50 percent the arc reaches the midpoint of every side and the four corners meet. The square becomes a circle.
blocks.9.text: 50 percent makes a circle only out of a square. Out of a rectangle it makes an ellipse.
blocks.11.text: With four numbers each corner gets its own, clockwise again, this time starting from the top left:
blocks.13.text: That is the form for a card header — rounded on top, square at the bottom so it meets the block below. There are long names for single corners too, if you only need one: border-top-left-radius and the other three.
blocks.15.text: Round the card by 8 pixels on all four corners. Make the badge a circle without changing its size — it is 64 by 64 and stays that way.
